title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: Similar results were found even among samples processed with a fixed centrifugation speed (R = 0.98, P ≤ 1.3 × 10−11; Fig. 1h)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: the median concentration was approximately 7.5-fold higher at 1,200g than at 2,500g ... average expression of platelet-specific genes ... was significantly associated with centrifugation speed ... the first principal component (PC1) captured 70% of expression variation and was strongly associated with platelet expression ... Similar results were found even among samples processed with a fixed centrifugation speed
outcome: Collectively, these results suggest that platelets represent the largest source of expression variation in cfRNA and that platelet contamination is a critical pre-analytical variable to control for in cfRNA analyses

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: whether expression contributions from platelet contamination could be algorithmically eliminated
context: Although individual platelets contain less RNA content than leukocytes (Ref: 13), platelets outnumber leukocytes by approximately two orders of magnitude in blood. In addition, physiological variation in platelet counts span an approximately threefold range in healthy adults (Ref: 14) with further variability observed in the context of cancer (Ref: 15). Therefore, even optimized methods to minimize plasma contamination by platelets could fail to completely remove platelet-derived RNA from cfRNA samples. Separately, from a practical standpoint, it is often not feasible to standardize blood banking protocols, especially when profiling previously collected samples.
outcome: we developed an in silico approach to remove unwanted gene expression driven by varying platelet contamination levels (Fig. 1i)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: the lack of the correlation between platelet expression and PC1 after correction (R = 0.07, P = 0.69; Fig. 1j)
outcome: this approach successfully removed the unwanted variation contributed by platelets

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: samples clustered according to platelet gene expression before correction (Fig. 1k), but according to inter-individual expression differences after correction (Fig. 1l)
outcome: Hierarchical clustering further confirmed this effect

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Platelet genes are enriched in cfRNA
main_content: the lack of the correlation between platelet expression and PC1 after correction ... samples clustered according to platelet gene expression before correction (Fig. 1k), but according to inter-individual expression differences after correction (Fig. 1l)
outcome: Therefore, addressing platelet contamination in cfRNA analyses is critical for reliably uncovering gene expression contributions from non-platelet sources

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: selectively capturing genes absent from or lowly expressed in healthy control cfRNA could enhance detection of non-haematopoietic tissue or disease signatures and expand the utility of RARE-seq to a wide range of clinical applications
context: Although cfRNA is highly enriched for expression of genes from non-haematopoietic sources, the absolute expression levels of these genes are relatively low compared with haematopoietic-derived transcripts (for example, globins) and highly expressed housekeeping genes (for example, β-actin).
outcome: we identified such ‘rare abundance genes’ (RAGs) using whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq data from healthy cfRNA samples (n = 50) and RNA-seq data from healthy whole-blood samples (n = 307) from the GTEx project (Ref: 16) (Fig. 2a)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: As compared with all protein-coding transcripts, RAGs were substantially enriched for both non-haematopoietic tissue-specific genes and cancer-specific genes from diverse cancers (Fig. 2b,c)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: The enrichment of genes specific for multiple tissue and cancer types among RAGs
outcome: suggests that a RAG-targeted sequencing panel could increase sensitivity for detection of cancer or other conditions of interest without requiring a bespoke panel design for each application

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: increase sensitivity for detection of cancer or other conditions of interest without requiring a bespoke panel design for each application
outcome: We therefore designed a capture panel targeting 4,737 RAGs and 50 housekeeping genes, prioritizing genes with relatively uniform expression across healthy cfRNA and whole-blood samples while covering a broad dynamic range of expression (Extended Data Fig. 3b)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: profiled five healthy cfRNA samples
outcome: using both whole-coding transcriptome and RAG capture panels using RARE-seq

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Gene expression was highly correlated between matched libraries (R = 0.98, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3e), including for the housekeeping genes (R = 0.97, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3f)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Within the subset of genes shared across both capture panels, RAG capture resulted in higher unique sequencing depth (Fig. 2d), more genes detected (Fig. 2e) and higher unique sequencing depth per gene detected (Fig. 2f)
outcome: Thus, targeted capture of RAGs effectively increases sensitivity for detection of genes that are lowly expressed in healthy cfRNA

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: determine the limit of 95% detection (LOD95) for transcriptional signatures of interest, using either whole-coding transcriptome or RAG capture panels
outcome: To establish the LOD95 of RARE-seq, we generated in silico serial dilutions of RNA from the NCI-H1975 NSCLC cell line into healthy control cfRNA at various cancer fractions

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: the LOD95 for RAG RARE-seq was 0.05% (Fig. 2h), more than 50-fold more sensitive than for whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq (2.8%; Fig. 2i)
outcome: demonstrating the value of targeting RAGs

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: the value of targeting RAGs
outcome: To experimentally confirm this result, we created in vitro dilution series, by spiking NCI-H1975 RNA into healthy donor cfRNA at defined concentrations

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: These in vitro spikes demonstrated strong agreement with our in silico results (Fig. 2h,i)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: evaluated the effect of sequencing coverage depth (range of 10–50 million reads) on the LOD95 of RAG-targeted RARE-seq and found that higher sequencing depth improved the LOD95 (Extended Data Fig. 3i)
outcome: Accordingly, we targeted 50 million reads for subsequent experiments using RARE-seq with the RAG panel

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: investigated performance for tumour-naive detection of lung adenocarcinomas (LUADs), the most common type of non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC)
outcome: We analysed cfRNA from 139 patients with LUAD ... and 50 controls without cancer

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Concentrations of cfRNA and sequencing depth were similar between groups (Extended Data Fig. 4a,b)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Platelet-adjusted differential expression analysis between LUADs and controls identified 96 genes overexpressed in LUADs, including key genes known to be highly expressed in LUADs such as SLC34A2, SFTA2 and NKX2-1 (also known as TTF1; Fig. 3a)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: LUAD DEGs were confirmed to be highly expressed in LUAD tumours from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (Ref: 17), whereas were depleted in meta-reference cfRNA controls (Fig. 3b)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: GSEA revealed LUAD cfRNA to be most enriched for epithelial cells including pulmonary alveolar type II cells, and most depleted for naive and memory B lymphocytes (Fig. 3c)
outcome: Thus, cfRNA from patients with LUAD is enriched for transcripts that are highly expressed in LUAD tumours

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: To further quantify the enrichment of LUAD-derived transcripts
outcome: we applied the cfRNA enrichment score framework developed above using a LUAD-specific gene signature (LUAD Sig; n = 72 genes) identified using RNA-seq data from LUAD tumours within TCGA (Ref: 17), LUAD cell lines from the Cancer Cell Line Encyclopedia (CCLE) (Ref: 18), whole blood (GTEx (Ref: 16)) and meta-reference cfRNA controls (Methods)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: The LUAD Sig was detected in 101 out of 139 participants with LUAD (73% sensitivity at 95% specificity in non-cancer controls; Fig. 3d and Supplementary Table 3) and was significantly associated with stage (for sensitivity by stage, I = 30%, II = 62%, III = 67% and IV = 83%; P = 9.1 × 10−16; Fig. 3e)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: the LUAD cfRNA enrichment score was significantly correlated with ctDNA variant allele frequency (R = 0.43, P = 0.009; Fig. 3f)
context: in a subset of plasma samples that were also interrogated using tumour-naive ctDNA analysis (n = 38; Methods)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Of these samples, 34% was detected by only RARE-seq and none was detected by only ctDNA analysis (Fig. 3g)
outcome: indicating that tumour-naive ctRNA analysis is more sensitive than tumour-naive ctDNA analysis

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Technical variables that varied across groups, such as library input and unique sequencing depth, extraction method and capture panel, were not associated with sensitivity (Extended Data Fig. 4e–h)
outcome: indicating that these variables did not drive detection

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: detection was not significantly associated with biological factors such as patient smoking status or the presence of oncogenic driver mutations (Extended Data Fig. 4i–k)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: we found that platelet correction significantly improved sensitivity (P = 0.03; Extended Data Fig. 4l)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: explored the ability to genotype cancer-derived somatic mutations using RARE-seq
context: Although non-invasive tumour genotyping is already routinely performed clinically using ctDNA and ctRNA-based genotyping is unlikely to outperform it, variant detection could be a useful addition to cfRNA analysis.
outcome: We therefore developed a custom ctRNA genotyping approach to detect clinically actionable variants, including single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions or deletions (indels), splice variants and gene fusions, in recurrent NSCLC oncogenes

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: ensure specificity
outcome: putative variants were censored if their variant allele frequency did not significantly exceed the background error rate observed in a subset of withheld controls (n = 50; Extended Data Fig. 5a; Methods)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: We detected one or more somatic driver mutations in 28% of patients with LUAD and 1% of controls, including key NSCLC oncogenic drivers such as L858R and exon 19 deletions in EGFR, G12C in KRAS, ROS1 and RET fusions, and MET exon skipping events (Fig. 3h,i and Extended Data Fig. 5b–d)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: Of the 162 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA or ctDNA genotyping in the same patients, 35 (22%) were also detectable in ctRNA, and detection rates were similar across variant types (Fig. 3j)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: Of the 34 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA in patients with matched cfRNA and cfDNA data available, 11 variants were detectable in ctDNA compared with 3 variants detectable in ctRNA
outcome: indicating that ctRNA genotyping may be useful to supplement expression-based analyses but should not replace ctDNA genotyping (Extended Data Fig. 5e)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: We observed a significant relationship between variant detection rates and corresponding expression levels; for instance, EGFR variants were more frequently detected in cfRNA samples with high EGFR expression (P = 0.0001; Fig. 3k)
outcome: Thus, RARE-seq analysis allows simultaneous identification of somatic mutations in patients with cancer

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: whether a machine learning approach could combine these features into a single detection algorithm
context: Given that ctRNA expression signatures and ctRNA somatic variation could individually distinguish between patients with cancer and controls without cancer
outcome: We trained a weighted elastic net classifier to distinguish LUAD and control cfRNA using tenfold nested cross-validation in a training cohort of 96 stage IV cases and 47 controls

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: The model demonstrated high classification performance (area under receiver operating characteristic curve, AUROC = 0.94; Fig. 3l,m and Supplementary Table 5) and selected 23 features including gene expression features (for example, key LUAD markers such as SLC34A2) and mutation-based features (that is, presence of L858R in EGFR or a KRAS mutation; Extended Data Fig. 5f)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: whether our cohort was sufficiently large
outcome: we repeated classifier training and cross-validation using sub-sampling

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: found stable performance when using at least 100 samples for model training (Extended Data Fig. 5g)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: The elastic net model performed similarly well in a withheld validation cohort collected at an independent institution (AUROC = 0.95; Fig. 3l,m)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA
main_content: Performance of the elastic net classifier was statistically similar to the enrichment score framework in the training cohort (P = 0.27), but improved performance in the validation cohort (P = 0.001)
outcome: Therefore, when sufficient samples are available, machine learning-based approaches can also be used to train robust classifiers for RARE-seq

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: in addition to identifying emergent somatic mutations mediating resistance, ctRNA analysis might allow detection of non-genetic therapeutic resistance mechanisms, such as histological transformation (Ref: 19)
context: Acquired resistance to EGFR TKIs is caused by heterogenous mechanisms, including both EGFR-dependent mechanisms (such as secondary point mutations in the EGFR gene) and EGFR-independent mechanisms (such as bypass pathway activation or histological transformation) (Ref: 20,21).
outcome: we focused on patients with EGFR-mutant LUAD treated with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: The mechanism of resistance was determined by tissue biopsy at the time of progression, revealing small-cell histological transformation in four patients, MET gene amplifications in three patients, and emergent C797S point mutations in EGFR in another three patients (Fig. 4a)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: At time of radiological progression, the SCLC Sig was detected in three of four (75%) patients with biopsy-proven histological transformation (Fig. 4b)
context: using a SCLC signature (SCLC Sig) defined using public tumour gene expression data (Ref: 18,22)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: observed decreased SCLC cfRNA enrichment score
context: analysed a plasma sample collected 274 days after the initiation of SCLC-targeted chemotherapy (for example, carboplatin/etoposide)
outcome: suggesting treatment response of the small cell component (Fig. 4c)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: assessed the performance of RARE-seq for identifying genetic mechanisms of resistance
outcome: To detect MET pathway activation caused by MET amplification using ctRNA, we developed a MET amplification signature (METamp Sig) containing nine genes differentially expressed in three EGFR-mutant NSCLC cell lines with acquired resistance due to MET amplification (Methods)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: The METamp Sig was detected in two of three (67%) patients with biopsy-proven MET amplification (Fig. 4d)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: we observed a decreased METamp cfRNA enrichment score in one patient after the MET inhibitor savolitinib was added to osimertinib, coincident with a partial response by imaging (Fig. 4e)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: We then identified EGFRC797S-mutant reads in two of three (67%) patients known to have acquired this mutation based on tumour DNA or ctDNA analysis (Fig. 4f,g)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: These resistance mechanisms were not detected in any pre-resistance timepoint
outcome: confirming the specificity of our approach (Supplementary Table 6)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: small-cell histological transformation ... MET gene amplifications ... emergent C797S point mutations in EGFR ... decreased SCLC cfRNA enrichment score ... METamp Sig was detected ... decreased METamp cfRNA enrichment score ... identified EGFRC797S-mutant reads ... not detected in any pre-resistance timepoint
outcome: Together, these results demonstrate proof of concept that RARE-seq can identify both non-genetic and genetic mechanisms underlying EGFR TKI resistance, including histological transformation, which is not detectable by mutation-based ctDNA approaches

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: whether the same RARE-seq assay could be used to detect other cancer types
outcome: We therefore analysed plasma samples from patients with three types of other carcinomas: pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD; n = 10 patients with stage IV), prostate adenocarcinoma (PRAD; n = 1 patient with stage III and n = 8 patients with stage IV) and liver hepatocellular carcinoma (LIHC; n = 3 patients with stage I, n = 5 patients with stage II and n = 2 patients with stage IV)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Sensitivity of cancer-specific cfRNA enrichment score detection was 80%, 90% and 100% for LIHC, PAAD and PRAD, respectively, after adjusting for multiple hypothesis testing (Fig. 5a)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: whether ctRNA analysis can distinguish between cancer types
context: Given that cancer-specific signatures were detectable in multiple cancer types ... The ability to identify the TOO of a malignancy non-invasively could be helpful for patients with advanced-stage cancers of unknown primary who have no clinically detected primary tumour.
outcome: we generated additional cancer signatures capturing genes uniquely expressed in each cancer compared with all other cancers, as well as to cfRNA from patients without cancer (cancer-TOO signatures)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: The top predicted TOO was correct 89% of the time and the top two predicted TOOs identified the correct tumour type 96% of the time (Fig. 5b,c)
outcome: Thus, cfRNA analysis could potentially enable non-invasive TOO identification

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: whether cfRNA expression might reveal lung injury caused by benign pulmonary conditions
outcome: We profiled cfRNA from 3 patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disorder, 7 patients with active COVID-19 infections (n = 13 timepoints) and 19 patients admitted to the intensive care unit with evidence of pulmonary injury (including 9 patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Across these diverse pulmonary conditions, lung-derived cfRNA was detectable in 49% of patients using a normal lung gene expression signature derived from GTEx (Ref: 16) (Fig. 5d and Supplementary Table 8)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: among adult participants without known pulmonary conditions (n = 42), lung cfRNA was detected in 14%, with detection being significantly more frequent in current smokers than in former- or never-smokers (45.5% versus 3.6%, P = 0.004; Fig. 5e)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: among adult participants without known pulmonary conditions (n = 42), lung cfRNA was detected in 14%, with detection being significantly more frequent in current smokers than in former- or never-smokers
outcome: This result suggests that lung cfRNA is induced by ongoing injury to pulmonary epithelium caused by recent exposure to tobacco smoke

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: in patients with known pulmonary conditions, detection was significantly greater in those who were on a ventilator at the time of blood collection (Fig. 5f)
outcome: probably reflecting the severity of the pulmonary condition as well as insult to pulmonary epithelium from mechanical ventilation

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: lung cfRNA is induced by ongoing injury to pulmonary epithelium caused by recent exposure to tobacco smoke ... probably reflecting the severity of the pulmonary condition as well as insult to pulmonary epithelium from mechanical ventilation
outcome: Thus, cfRNA analysis may have utility for assessment of benign conditions involving acute and chronic patterns of tissue injury

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: whether RARE-seq might have utility for simultaneously tracking RNA-based treatments and their effects on the host
context: given the rapidly growing interest in RNA-based vaccines and therapeutics
outcome: we longitudinally profiled plasma from five individuals receiving mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccinations (n = 20 timepoints)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Vaccine RNA was detectable immediately after vaccination and persisted for up to 6 weeks after injection (Fig. 5g)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Host genes that were differentially expressed after vaccination were enriched for inflammatory responses, interferon-γ responses and cytokine signalling
outcome: reflecting immune activation in response to vaccination (Fig. 5h)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Vaccine RNA and host immune response genes were both highest in the first week post-vaccination (Fig. 5i)
outcome: (N/A)

title: An ultrasensitive method for detection of cell-free RNA
main_content: Vaccine RNA was detectable immediately after vaccination and persisted for up to 6 weeks after injection ... Host genes that were differentially expressed after vaccination were enriched for inflammatory responses ... Vaccine RNA and host immune response genes were both highest in the first week post-vaccination
outcome: These results highlight the potential of RARE-seq for simultaneously measuring pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic aspects of RNA-based therapies
